3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Singh, G., Engel, O. A., Haddad, A., Smith, N., Shu, S., Baker, F. C., & Ganson, K. T. (2025). Social media use and depressive symptoms during early adolescence. JAMA Network Open, 8(4), e2511704.
3A_Bibliographic.notes: Authors inferred from standard JAMA Network Open format; senior author listed as last author.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Sociodemographic characteristics of the analytic sample after excluding participants with missing baseline data for age or sex assigned at birth (N = 11 876)
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Sample sizes varied across waves due to attrition and missing data. FIML used for missing data.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Social media use (weighted mean daily hours)
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous hours/day (weighted mean: [(weekday mean x 5) + (weekend mean x 2)]/7)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Social media use was defined for this analysis as time spent on social media daily and was assessed using the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey administered each year. Adolescents responded to questions about the number of hours and minutes per weekday and weekend day they spent engaging with social media. The total time spent on social media was calculated as the weighted sum: [(weekday mean x 5) + (weekend mean x 2)]/7. Weighted mean social media use was reported as a continuous variable, representing the mean daily time spent (in hours).

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Depressive symptoms (CBCL depressive problems raw score)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) DSM-oriented depressive problems subscale
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Depressive symptoms were assessed using the validated Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) depressive problems score (from the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders-oriented scales) each year as reported by the caregiver. The raw score (as opposed to the t score) was chosen to capture the full distribution of symptom severity in a community-based sample.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Random-intercept cross-lagged panel model (RI-CLPM), unconstrained, estimated with MLR in lavaan
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: sex, race and ethnicity, household income, highest parental educational level, adverse childhood experiences, parental monitoring scale, family conflict, study site
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: To examine the reciprocal associations between social media use and depressive symptoms over 4 time points, we fit several longitudinal structural equation models. We first estimated a traditional cross-lagged panel model (CLPM) and then compared it with random-intercept CLPMs (RI-CLPMs). All models were estimated using maximum likelihood with robust SEs (MLR) in the lavaan package in R, version 4.4.2. Two-sided P < .05 was considered significant.
3E_Design_Analysis.notes: In accordance with benchmarks of Orth et al, cross-lagged effect sizes were interpreted as small (beta=0.03), medium (beta=0.07), and large (beta=0.12).

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Between-person covariance of social media use and depressive symptoms (random intercepts)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: There were no between-person associations between depressive symptoms and social media use, as evidenced by the covariance between the latent random intercepts (beta, -0.01; 95% CI, -0.04 to 0.02; P = .46).
social media use at baseline and depressive symptoms in year 1 (beta, 0.03; 95% CI, -0.02 to 0.09; P = .19)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Within-person cross-lagged path (RI-CLPM); medium effect size per Orth et al benchmarks
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: from year 1 to year 2, social media use higher than the person-level mean in year 1 was associated with greater depressive symptoms in year 2 (beta, 0.07; 95% CI, 0.01-0.12; P = .01; medium effect size)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Within-person cross-lagged path (RI-CLPM); medium effect size per Orth et al benchmarks
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: social media use higher than the person-level mean in year 2 was positively associated with greater depressive symptoms in year 3 (beta, 0.09; 95% CI, 0.04-0.14; P < .001; medium effect size)
no association between depressive symptoms at baseline and social media use in year 1 (beta, 0.00; 95% CI, -0.01 to 0.02; P = .46)
year 1 depressive symptoms and year 2 social media use: beta, 0.00 [95% CI, -0.007 to 0.01]; P = .65
year 2 depressive symptoms and year 3 social media use: (beta, 0.00 [95% CI, -0.003 to 0.02]; P = .18)

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Contemporaneous social media use and depressive symptoms at year 3
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: At year 3, the standardized residual covariance between social media use and depressive symptoms was 0.07 (95% CI, 0.03-0.10; P < .001)

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: In accordance with the benchmarks of Orth et al, cross-lagged effect sizes were interpreted as small (beta = 0.03), medium (beta = 0.07), and large (beta = 0.12).
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper uses calibrated effect size benchmarks from Orth et al. for RI-CLPM cross-lagged paths. The reported cross-lagged effects of 0.07 and 0.09 are described as 'medium' using these benchmarks. Abstract is descriptive without significance language.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Clinicians should consider inquiring about social media use among children and adolescents, particularly those younger than the recommended age limits (the minimum age requirement for most social media platforms is 13 years), and providing anticipatory guidance as needed. Professional organizations, such as the American Academy of Pediatrics, could refine guidelines on social media use.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion frames the within-person RI-CLPM findings as providing 'initial evidence of temporal ordering' suggesting social media use may be a 'potential contributing factor' to depressive symptoms. It acknowledges the observational design and discusses findings within the Differential Susceptibility to Media Effects Model (DSMM). The paper recommends clinical screening and mindful social media use, despite effects being in the small-to-medium range by the study's own cited benchmarks.
